title: How to Fix Loose Glasses: DIY Adjustments and When to See a Pro
description: Step-by-step guide to tightening loose glasses at home. Learn quick fixes for slipping frames, bent temples, and loose nose pads — plus when to visit an optician.
tldr: Most loose glasses can be temporarily fixed by tightening the hinge screws with a small screwdriver or gently bending the temple arms inward with warm water. For lasting repairs, visit an optician — adjustments are usually free.

definition.text: A glasses adjustment is the process of modifying the fit of eyeglass frames by tightening screws, bending temples, or reshaping nose pads so the glasses sit securely and comfortably on the wearer's face.

sections.content:
Glasses loosen over time for a few predictable reasons. The tiny hinge screws gradually back out from the constant open-close motion. Metal and acetate frames slowly lose their shape from body heat, being set down on surfaces, and the occasional drop. Nose pads flatten and compress after months of daily wear.

If your glasses are relatively new and already feel loose, they may not have been fitted properly in the first place. A proper fitting during your eye exam should set the starting point, but frames often need a minor tune-up after a couple of weeks of wear.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: Quick Fix #1: Tighten the Hinge Screws
sections.content:
This solves the problem about 60% of the time. You'll need a small Phillips or flathead screwdriver — the kind that comes in eyeglass repair kits (available at any pharmacy for a few dollars).

  1. Hold the frame steady on a flat surface.
  2. Locate the small screw on each hinge (where the temple arm connects to the front).
  3. Turn clockwise gently — just until snug. Over-tightening can strip the screw or crack the frame.
  4. Open and close the temples to test. They should move smoothly with slight resistance.

If a screw keeps loosening, a tiny drop of clear nail polish on the threads acts as a mild thread-locker. Let it dry before reinserting.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: Quick Fix #2: Adjust the Temple Arms
sections.content:
If your glasses slide down your nose even with tight screws, the temple arms (the pieces that hook behind your ears) probably need reshaping.

For Metal Frames

Gently bend the temple tips inward (toward your head) and increase the downward curve behind the ear. Use your fingers — no tools needed. Make small adjustments and try them on between each bend.

For Acetate (Plastic) Frames

Acetate becomes pliable with heat. Run the temple arm under warm water (not boiling) for 20–30 seconds, then bend gently. Hold the new shape for 15 seconds while it cools. Never use a hair dryer or microwave — uneven heat can warp or discolor the frame.

The goal is to have the temples follow the contour of your head with light, even pressure. They shouldn't pinch behind your ears or leave red marks.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: Quick Fix #3: Adjust the Nose Pads
sections.content:
Glasses with adjustable nose pads (common on metal frames) can be fine-tuned easily:

  • Glasses sit too low: Push the nose pads closer together (inward).
  • Glasses sit too high: Spread the nose pads farther apart.
  • Glasses tilt to one side: Adjust the pad arm on the side that sits lower — push it in slightly.

Use your thumbs and index fingers, applying gentle pressure to the pad arms (not the pads themselves). Acetate frames with built-in nose bridges can't be adjusted this way — that's a job for a professional with a heat gun.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: When to Skip the DIY and Visit an Optician
sections.content:
Home fixes are fine for minor looseness, but some situations call for professional help:

  • A screw is missing — opticians stock replacement screws in every size and can match yours in seconds.
  • The frame is bent or twisted — straightening a warped frame without proper tools risks snapping it.
  • One lens pops out — the groove or rim may be damaged and need repair.
  • Nose pads are cracked or yellowed — cheap to replace at an optical shop.
  • Repeated loosening — could indicate a stripped screw hole or worn hinge that needs a different fix.

Most optical stores offer free adjustments, even if you didn't buy your glasses there. Charm Optical in South Edmonton does free adjustments and minor repairs — you can walk in anytime during store hours.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

sections.h2: Preventing Loose Glasses in the First Place
sections.content:
A few habits go a long way:

  • Use both hands to take off your glasses. Pulling from one side stretches that temple arm.
  • Store them in a case when not wearing them — setting them face-down on a nightstand is asking for trouble.
  • Get regular tune-ups — stop in for a quick adjustment every few months, especially if you wear your glasses all day.
  • Don't push them up by the lens — push from the bridge. Lens-pushing bends the frame unevenly and smudges your lenses.
  • Keep them away from heat — car dashboards in Alberta summers will warp acetate frames fast.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. sections.h2: Essential Tools for At-Home Glasses Repair
sections.content:
If you wear glasses daily, keep a small repair kit handy. Here's what to include:

ToolPurposeCost
Micro screwdriver setTightening hinge screws$3–$8
Spare screws (assorted)Replacing lost screwsIncluded in kits
Microfiber clothCleaning lenses during repair$2–$5
Nose pad replacementsSwapping worn pads$5–$10
Clear nail polishThread-locking loose screws$2

You can find eyeglass repair kits at most pharmacies, dollar stores, or online for under $10. They're worth keeping in a desk drawer or travel bag.

If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Can I use super glue to fix loose glasses?
faqs.a: Avoid super glue on glasses — it bonds permanently, can fog lenses with fumes, and makes professional repair much harder afterward. Use clear nail polish on screw threads if needed, or take them to an optician for a proper fix.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to get glasses adjusted at a store?
faqs.a: Most optical stores adjust glasses for free, including Charm Optical in Edmonton. Even if you bought your glasses elsewhere, basic adjustments like tightening and reshaping are typically complimentary.

faqs.q: My glasses keep sliding down my nose — what helps?
faqs.a: Tighten the hinge screws first. If that doesn't work, bend the temple tips inward slightly and increase the curve behind your ears. For nose-pad frames, push the pads closer together. Silicone nose pad covers (stick-on) also add grip for a quick fix.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses professionally adjusted?
faqs.a: Every 3–4 months is a good rhythm for daily wearers. Stop in sooner if you notice slipping, uneven sitting, or pressure points. It takes about 5 minutes and keeps your glasses comfortable and your vision clear.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Scratched lenses cannot be repaired — the scratch is in the coating or lens material itself. You'll need new lenses. Many single vision lenses are available same-day at Charm Optical in Edmonton, and most insurance plans cover lens replacement every 1-2 years.
